paragraphs.text: The Ada Lovelace Institute commends the European Commission's initiative to make the EU's consumer protection laws fit for purpose and ensure protection in an ever-changing digital environment. Our mission is to ensure data and AI work for people and society and our work across the UK and the EU considers how the interests of people and society are met by data, digital and AI regulation.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Introduction

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns are still prevalent
user personalised AI systems

paragraphs.text: Questions around digital fairness are becoming increasingly relevant. While well researched forms of unfair practices, like dark patterns are still prevalent, new ways of exercising influence over consumers are emerging, with the rise of increasingly personable and user personalised AI systems, which we refer to as Advanced AI Assistants. Over the past year, the Ada Lovelace Institute has been conducting research on the development of and risks associated with these 'Advanced AI Assistants' which we have found to pose significant consumer protection risks.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Introduction

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns and other deceptive practices around online privacy.

paragraphs.text: This submission outlines our concerns about the novel consumer protection risks posed by the development of 'Advanced AI Assistants', as well as a section outlining additional concerns around digital fairness relating to dark patterns and other deceptive practices around online privacy.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Introduction

paragraphs.text: The Digital Fairness Act is an extremely timely initiative that could be material in ensuring the effective protection of consumer rights in the EU for the years to come. This is an opportunity to proactively update consumer protection laws to make privacy protections online more robust and address the concerns raised by Advanced AI Assistants. At the same time, failure to adequately address these issues could lead to significant harms to EU consumers and the wider economy over time and would be a significant missed opportunity for this legislative window.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Introduction

paragraphs.text: Advanced AI Assistants as digital mediators to information and products

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Advanced AI Assistants

paragraphs.text: What are Advanced AI Assistants?

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Advanced AI Assistants

paragraphs.text: Over the past few years, a new, powerful class of AI systems has emerged. The Ada Lovelace Institute refers to these systems as Advanced AI Assistants. These systems

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Advanced AI Assistants

evidence_quotes.quote: highly personalized to their users.

paragraphs.text: can talk to their users, produce complex responses on the basis of those conversations, and are typically highly personable and highly personalized to their users. Advanced AI Assistants ('Assistants') are closely related to, but not the same as AI agents, as AI agents are defined by their ability to take goal directed, autonomous action and do not need to have a natural language interface.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Advanced AI Assistants

evidence_quotes.quote: personalisation

paragraphs.text: Because of their combination of 'personality' and personalisation, Assistants can be very easy for users to anthropomorphise, form emotional attachments with, and to trust. In many cases, this could make Assistants very persuasive, meaning their outputs could meaningfully influence human action or decision-making.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Advanced AI Assistants

evidence_quotes.quote: provide personalised answers to user's queries
may be used to recommend product and services to users

paragraphs.text: Adoption of Assistants is expected to set to proliferate quickly. Their ease of use and increasingly wide availability makes them appealing to both businesses and individual users. Assistants can also be used for a wider variety of tasks compared to earlier forms of AI. For example, Assistants may be used to recommend product and services to users, provide personalised answers to user's queries, and to offer emotional support to users. Although some Assistants will be specialist systems and geared towards just one of these tasks, others will be general use.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Advanced AI Assistants

paragraphs.text: All this together means that Assistants could become the principal means by which most people access the internet and interact with the digital world – as universal digital intermediaries – giving them substantial influencing power over users.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Advanced AI Assistants

paragraphs.text: How can Advanced AI Assistants influence consumers?

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Advanced AI Assistants

evidence_quotes.quote: Over time, Assistants can become personalized to their user in the tone of voice they use, the ideas they support, and the products they recommend.
they require large amounts of users' personal data
They may also probe users for information that might otherwise not be digitally accessible.

paragraphs.text: Over time, Assistants can become personalized to their user in the tone of voice they use, the ideas they support, and the products they recommend. For Assistants to work as intended they require large amounts of users' personal data, and may use their trusted relationship with the user to get them to divulge personal information. They may also probe users for information that might otherwise not be digitally accessible.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Advanced AI Assistants

evidence_quotes.quote: powerful tools for manipulation.

paragraphs.text: The perceived ‘trustworthiness’ and ‘helpfulness’ of an Assistant invites reliance by the user on the information, advice and recommendations they receive from their Assistant. Overall, this can foster practical and emotional dependency in users, as well as making Assistants powerful tools for manipulation. These risks are particularly acute for minors and vulnerable groups in society but are relevant for all age groups and demographics.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Advanced AI Assistants

evidence_quotes.quote: Now, there is a technology that fosters a trust-based relationship with its users simulating a trusted human relationship that can influence their decisions more profoundly.

paragraphs.text: The development of Advanced AI Assistants and their potential use for wide-ranging tasks and questions presents a new kind of challenge for the realm of consumer protection. Before, a website or an algorithm may have nudged a consumer towards a certain vendor or to (quickly!) complete a purchase. Now, there is a technology that fosters a trust-based relationship with its users simulating a trusted human relationship that can influence their decisions more profoundly.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Advanced AI Assistants

paragraphs.text: This is not limited to the kinds of Assistants that can complete purchases on a user’s behalf (agents/executors). Instead, Assistants can exercise influence over consumers at every stage of the purchasing process:

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Advanced AI Assistants

evidence_quotes.quote: creating a need that was not there before

paragraphs.text: The Assistant can recommend that the user needs a product thereby creating a need that was not there before;

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Advanced AI Assistants

evidence_quotes.quote: curate the list of options, determining the products a user does and does not consider.

paragraphs.text: The Assistant can find options for purchasing a product from one or more vendors, and can curate the list of options, determining the products a user does and does not consider.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Advanced AI Assistants

evidence_quotes.quote: The Assistant controls how those product options are presented to the user and what information about the products is shared;

paragraphs.text: The Assistant controls how those product options are presented to the user and what information about the products is shared;

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Advanced AI Assistants

evidence_quotes.quote: The Assistant can contact the seller, and in some cases complete the purchase on the consumer’s behalf.

paragraphs.text: The Assistant can contact the seller, and in some cases complete the purchase on the consumer’s behalf.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Advanced AI Assistants

evidence_quotes.quote: intervene early in the consumer’s decision-making process (or indeed, even prompt the consumer to consider buying a product)

paragraphs.text: The fact that users may have developed a dependency on the Assistant coupled with the Assistant’s ability to intervene early in the consumer’s decision-making process (or indeed, even prompt the consumer to consider buying a product), and exercise influence over the consumer at various stages of this process makes the risk for undue influence particularly acute. Assistants thus enhance scale, speed, precision, and potency of how consumers can be targeted.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Advanced AI Assistants

evidence_quotes.quote: Large potential for ‘personalised persuasion based on behavioural data’

paragraphs.text: Large potential for ‘personalised persuasion based on behavioural data’

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Advanced AI Assistants

evidence_quotes.quote: requiring user's data to personalise interactions with their user

paragraphs.text: Assistants are a data hungry technology, requiring user's data to personalise interactions with their user and, in some cases, use such data to further develop its underlying model's capabilities. As Assistants come to play the role of trusted advisors, assistants and confidants, their users could feel more comfortable sharing personal details that they would otherwise not share. Additionally, many Assistants (particularly ‘agents’) require access to and control of user’s devices, accounts and software to function.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Advanced AI Assistants

evidence_quotes.quote: for personalized persuasion and targeting of consumers.

paragraphs.text: This far-reaching access to users’ information and data makes Assistants a prime technology to use for personalized persuasion and targeting of consumers. The ‘profile’ that the Assistant is able to build up on the user will have a level of depth and detail that other technologies are unlikely to have access to. On the one hand, this may lead to consumers being offered more relevant products that better meet their needs. On the other hand, given that Assistants are likely to control which products the user gets to see, this can be abused to:

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Advanced AI Assistants

evidence_quotes.quote: Offer products at personalised prices;

paragraphs.text: Offer products at personalised prices;

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Advanced AI Assistants

evidence_quotes.quote: appeal to the consumer’s personal preferences

paragraphs.text: Describe or list products in ways (whether accurate or not) that appeal to the consumer’s personal preferences;

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Advanced AI Assistants

evidence_quotes.quote: Target consumers with suggestions for products and services at moments when they’re more likely to be convinced to buy something (when they’re tired, hungry or otherwise more vulnerable).

paragraphs.text: Target consumers with suggestions for products and services at moments when they’re more likely to be convinced to buy something (when they’re tired, hungry or otherwise more vulnerable).

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Advanced AI Assistants

evidence_quotes.quote: The fact that practices around personalization may not be transparent to the consumer raises possible legal concerns.
personalised pricing without explicitly informing consumers is unfair

paragraphs.text: The fact that practices around personalization may not be transparent to the consumer raises possible legal concerns. Based on EU consumer law applying personalised pricing without explicitly informing consumers is unfair ‘as it hinders them from making an informed choice’. Furthermore, additional legal concerns would apply if legally protected characteristics of consumers or sensitive consumer data are considered in the personalised pricing practice.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Advanced AI Assistants

evidence_quotes.quote: personalised pricing

paragraphs.text: Moreover, the potential of Assistants to lead to a significant expansion of personalised pricing is also a cause for concern. As well as being unpopular with consumers,

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Advanced AI Assistants

evidence_quotes.quote: predictions about the maximum amount they are willing to pay
can be used to exploit vulnerable consumes.

paragraphs.text: the same item based on predictions about the maximum amount they are willing to pay – can undermine consumers' ability to understand the real price of a product, distort market competition, can be used to exploit vulnerable consumes.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Advanced AI Assistants

evidence_quotes.quote: It is generally not clear to consumers how Assistants arrive at their recommendations.

paragraphs.text: It is generally not clear to consumers how Assistants arrive at their recommendations. Although consumers – due to the perceived ‘trusted’ relationship – are likely to assume the Assistant is working ‘for them’, or is acting to their benefit, questions around value alignment of Assistants are currently unsolved.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Problems with opacity and evidence

evidence_quotes.quote: opacity of Assistant’s decision-making and sourcing of information makes it difficult to ascertain whether hidden commercial interests may influence the product that is recommended, where the Assistant may be working for the interests of the AI developer or a third party.

paragraphs.text: In a less nefarious scenario, Assistants are prone to misunderstanding the values and objectives of users or may be exposed to poorly articulated requests or goals. This may lead to the Assistant pursuing outcomes that are not aligned with the interests of the user. Moreover, the opacity of Assistant’s decision-making and sourcing of information makes it difficult to ascertain whether hidden commercial interests may influence the product that is recommended, where the Assistant may be working for the interests of the AI developer or a third party. Elon Musk recently announced that the chatbot of his company, Grok, will start to introduce ads into their response to queries.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Problems with opacity and evidence

evidence_quotes.quote: Assistants simply give the answer that their user wants to hear
not necessarily what is ‘true’

paragraphs.text: Even if prompted ‘why’ the Assistant recommends a certain product, the answer may not always be truthful or transparent about potential hidden motives: Assistants simply give the answer that their user wants to hear, or answers that have been reinforced in its training process, not necessarily what is ‘true’. ¹²

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Problems with opacity and evidence

evidence_quotes.quote: This is not transparent to the consumer

paragraphs.text: This means that Assistants do not necessarily always act in line with the best interest of the consumer, either unintentionally or because the Assistant is promoting the interests of commercial actors. This is not transparent to the consumer, and if the consumer somehow does sense that the Assistant is not working in their best interest, this is incredibly hard to evidence.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Problems with opacity and evidence

evidence_quotes.quote: Advanced AI Assistants exhibit a serious potential to impact on consumer’s transactional decisions: Assistants are primed to develop a relationship of emotional

paragraphs.text: Advanced AI Assistants exhibit a serious potential to impact on consumer’s transactional decisions: Assistants are primed to develop a relationship of emotional

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Need for clarification and updating of provisions

evidence_quotes.quote: provide product recommendations to the user and influence their decision-making.

paragraphs.text: and practical reliance with their user and are positioned to provide product recommendations to the user and influence their decision-making.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Need for clarification and updating of provisions

evidence_quotes.quote: interface design and functionality decisions in the development of virtual assistants

paragraphs.text: The Digital Fairness Check Report recognizes that ‘undue influence’ can be ‘exercised in myriad of ways, e.g. through interface design and functionality decisions in the development of virtual assistants’. Although the definition of ‘undue influence’ may allow for an interpretation that includes undue influence by Advanced AI Assistants through exploitation of their trusted relationship with their user, there is currently no legal certainty on whether the legal concept of ‘undue influence’ covers this, and to what extent. CJEU case law on this concept is sparse and does not provide sufficient clarification.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Need for clarification and updating of provisions

evidence_quotes.quote: design choices that involve anthropomorphic cues which are aimed at inducing an emotional connection with the user.

paragraphs.text: To address this, we recommend that binding guidance on the concept of ‘undue influence’ is issued that clearly recognises that undue influence can stem from choices around interface design, particularly design choices that involve anthropomorphic cues which are aimed at inducing an emotional connection with the user. We further recommend clarifying that in the definition of ‘undue influence’, the section on ‘exploiting a position of power’ can refer to the exploitation of an emotional or trusted relationship between an Advanced AI Assistant and their user. Similarly, ‘pressure’ within the definition of ‘undue influence’ should include the abuse of a trusted connection with a consumer to materially distort their economic behaviour, even if the consumer themselves does not experience the influence exercised over them by an Assistant as forceful or oppressive.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Need for clarification and updating of provisions

evidence_quotes.quote: not always clear to the consumer on what basis certain products are recommended
in what way the presentation or price of purchase options has been personalised
how product recommendations have been tailored to them.
it should always be clearly signposted whenever a message from an Assistant includes paid advertising
so it is transparent to the consumer when there is a commercial interest behind an Assistant’s recommendation.

paragraphs.text: Further, it is not always clear to the consumer on what basis certain products are recommended, or in what way the presentation or price of purchase options has been personalised. An additional duty to disclose essential information should be imposed on the providers of Advanced AI Assistants: this should allow consumers to access an understandable explanation of how product recommendations have been tailored to them. Additionally, it should always be clearly signposted whenever a message from an Assistant includes paid advertising, so it is transparent to the consumer when there is a commercial interest behind an Assistant’s recommendation.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Need for clarification and updating of provisions

evidence_quotes.quote: exploiting a consumer’s vulnerable moments (e.g. exhaustion, illness) with product placements
consumers should have the option to easily ‘turn off’ these recommendations in an app’s settings.

paragraphs.text: In order to prevent Assistants from exploiting a consumer’s vulnerable moments (e.g. exhaustion, illness) with product placements, consumers should have the option to easily ‘turn off’ these recommendations in an app’s settings. When turned off, an Assistant would not provide product recommendations or suggest the purchase of any product unless specifically queried by the consumer. Any regulation intended to ensure

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Need for clarification and updating of provisions

evidence_quotes.quote: ‘pay or consent’ business models for Assistants – in which a consumer of an otherwise free Assistants would have to pay for access to a version that does not make product recommendations.

paragraphs.text: consumers' ability to interact with Assistants without product recommendations should be worded to explicitly prohibit ‘pay or consent’ business models for Assistants – in which a consumer of an otherwise free Assistants would have to pay for access to a version that does not make product recommendations.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Need for clarification and updating of provisions

paragraphs.text: Dark patterns in pushing consumer to opt for (higher) ‘paid’ subscription tiers

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Business models of Advanced AI Assistants

evidence_quotes.quote: Assistants’ ability to exercise influence over the user to influence purchasing decisions on products from other sellers

paragraphs.text: Besides Assistants’ ability to exercise influence over the user to influence purchasing decisions on products from other sellers, there are also consumer protection concerns related to the business models of the AI companies themselves.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Business models of Advanced AI Assistants

evidence_quotes.quote: addictive features
‘premium’ subscriptions that allow users to access explicit content or unlimited interactions.

paragraphs.text: Many AI Assistants offer a free version of the product, albeit with restrictions on certain features or the amount of messages (or ‘tokens’) that can be exchanged within a certain time frame. Subsequently Assistant platforms offer ‘premium’ subscriptions that allow users to access explicit content or unlimited interactions. The practice of first getting people ‘hooked’ on the service through engaging and addictive features (voice mode, sycophantic conversation, anthropomorphic cues) but then limiting interactions with ‘their’ Assistant has the potential to emotionally pressure people to pay money to increase or retain access to their Assistant. Although using addictive features to get people to pay for premium models to retain access to a service may also be present in other services, like game subscriptions, the added element of the Assistant emulating a human in its interaction with the user and building up of an emotional relationship makes the Assistant context of particular concern.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Business models of Advanced AI Assistants

evidence_quotes.quote: these design choices should be considered a ‘dark pattern’

paragraphs.text: Through these design choices around the interface, features, and interaction patterns, AI companies are putting considerable pressure on consumers to pay (more) for their Assistant service. We recommend that these design choices should be considered a ‘dark pattern’, as the affective cues and features of Assistants’ design can impair consumers’ ability to make an informed decision.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Business models of Advanced AI Assistants

evidence_quotes.quote: manipulative practices

paragraphs.text: Given the challenges introduced by new forms of AI for consumer protection, manipulative practices, and extractive data gathering practices, it is key that the Digital Fairness Act is accompanied by strong legal standards that protect consumer’s privacy and data online. As Advanced AI Assistants, and particularly AI Agents, usually have deep access to user’s (sensitive) data and their devices (soft- and hardware) to function, it is important that provisions providing limitations how such data is

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Digital Fairness and Privacy Protections

evidence_quotes.quote: restrictions on what data Assistants and other consumer facing AI systems can collect

paragraphs.text: processed – and around how system level access is exploited – are strengthened rather than weakened. (Regulators may also want to consider introducing restrictions on what data Assistants and other consumer facing AI systems can collect, and the system level assess they are able to enjoy.) In this context, we are concerned by some of the simplification efforts towards the ePrivacy Directive and GDPR.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Digital Fairness and Privacy Protections

evidence_quotes.quote: deceptive and extractive practices

paragraphs.text: Article 5(3) prevents the storage of information on devices or access to that stored information without consent, unless access or storage is strictly necessary for providing the service which the user requested. This covers both personal and non-personal data. If Assistants perform tasks on behalf of the users, it should not be assumed that explicit, informed, freely given consent is implied. ePrivacy rules must be further strengthened to clarify protections in light of Assistants, and should require explicit consent. Simplification of Art. 5(3) ePrivacy ignores underlying issues with deceptive and extractive practices that are particularly acute in the age of AI.

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Digital Fairness and Privacy Protections

evidence_quotes.quote: free of dark patterns

paragraphs.text: Further, the GDPR covers the processing of personal data through cookies or other tracking technologies potentially placed by Assistants. The notion of personal data is intentionally broad: many data points (device settings, IDs, measurements, analytics) can become personal when combined with other information. Given the invasive nature of data gathering in the context of Assistants and other data hungry AI systems, we recommend that such protections are maintained. Online forms that ask consent for the processing of online data or tracking of data for advertising purposes ('cookie banners') should be free of dark patterns to ensure explicit, informed, and freely given consent is given (see below).

section_path: Ada Lovelace Institute's response to the Digital Fairness Act Call for Evidence
Digital Fairness and Privacy Protections

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns are well researched

paragraphs.text: General concerns around dark patterns are well researched and persist. Dark patterns continue to plague solutions that are meant to empower consumers and allow them to control how their data is being used. Some common examples include:

section_path: Dark Patterns and Privacy Consent Forms

evidence_quotes.quote: Default settings being preselected to the least privacy friendly option, requiring active user intervention to change this.[16]

paragraphs.text: Default settings being preselected to the least privacy friendly option, requiring active user intervention to change this.[16]

section_path: Dark Patterns and Privacy Consent Forms

evidence_quotes.quote: Making the privacy friendly option more cumbersome to select, for example by giving a 'one click' option to agree to data processing but multi-click route to opting out.[17]

paragraphs.text: Making the privacy friendly option more cumbersome to select, for example by giving a 'one click' option to agree to data processing but multi-click route to opting out.[17]

section_path: Dark Patterns and Privacy Consent Forms

evidence_quotes.quote: Nudging users to select the least privacy friendly option in a consent form by framing not consenting to data processing and tracking as being risky, or significantly impairing the service.

paragraphs.text: Nudging users to select the least privacy friendly option in a consent form by framing not consenting to data processing and tracking as being risky, or significantly impairing the service.

section_path: Dark Patterns and Privacy Consent Forms

evidence_quotes.quote: Deceptive design of the consent form itself by using deceptive links, colours, or buttons that ‘trick’ the consumer into selecting the consent option.

paragraphs.text: Deceptive design of the consent form itself by using deceptive links, colours, or buttons that ‘trick’ the consumer into selecting the consent option.

section_path: Dark Patterns and Privacy Consent Forms

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns

paragraphs.text: We recommend stronger enforcement in the context of dark patterns that influence consumer’s choices to share their data with platform providers and third parties.

section_path: Dark Patterns and Privacy Consent Forms

evidence_quotes.quote: Strong privacy protections are the backbone of consumer protection in the digital age.

Welcome initiatives such as the Digital Fairness Act should be situated in a broader strengthening of privacy and data protection laws and their effective enforcement to truly protect consumers in the age of AI.

paragraphs.text: Strong privacy protections are the backbone of consumer protection in the digital age. Welcome initiatives such as the Digital Fairness Act should be situated in a broader strengthening of privacy and data protection laws and their effective enforcement to truly protect consumers in the age of AI.

paragraphs.text: The Ada Lovelace Institute was established by the Nuffield Foundation in early 2018, in collaboration with The Alan Turing Institute, the Royal Society, the British Academy, the Royal Statistical Society, the Wellcome Trust, Luminate, techUK and the Nuffield Council on Bioethics.

section_path: About the Ada Lovelace Institute

paragraphs.text: The mission of the Ada Lovelace Institute is to ensure that data and AI work for people and society. We believe that a world where data and AI work for people and society is a world in which the opportunities, benefits and privileges generated by data and AI are justly and equitably distributed and experienced.

section_path: About the Ada Lovelace Institute

paragraphs.text: We recognise the power asymmetries that exist in ethical and legal debates around the development of data-driven technologies, and will represent people in those conversations. We focus not on the types of technologies we want to build, but on the types of societies we want to build. Through research, policy and practice, we aim to ensure that the transformative power of data and AI is used and harnessed in ways that maximise social wellbeing and put technology at the service of humanity.

section_path: About the Ada Lovelace Institute

paragraphs.text: We are funded by the Nuffield Foundation, an independent charitable trust with a mission to advance social well-being. The Foundation funds research that informs social policy, primarily in education, welfare and justice. In addition to the Ada Lovelace Institute, the Foundation is also the founder and co-funder of the Nuffield Council on Bioethics and the Nuffield Family Justice Observatory.
